images.caption: Figure 1:(a) Token count comparison before and after manually extracting effective text from 50 sampled trajectories on TB 2.0. (b) Performance comparison of the baseline agent, TACO, and static compression baselines on TB 2.0. “Seed Rules” rely on 6 predefined heuristics for general scenarios, while “High-Quality Rules” utilize 200 human-curated rules. Detailed rule specifications are provided in AppendixB.1.

Figure 2:Overview of TACO. For each task, TACO initializes active rules from the global rule pool (Rule Initialization part), compresses terminal outputs using the rule evolved within the task (Terminal output Compression part), and writes effective rules back to the global rule pool after completion (Global Rule Pool Updating part).

Figure 3:Agent Accuracy Under Identical Token Budgets. Across a range of fixed token budgets, we compare the accuracy achieved by Baseline and TACO. On all six models, TACO consistently attains higher accuracy than Baseline at the same token cost.

Figure 4:Pass@k comparison between Baseline and TACO across six models on TerminalBench 2.0. The top row shows large models, and the bottom row shows smaller Qwen3 models. TACO consistently improves pass@k across all settings.

Figure 5:Rule-frontier convergence and performance stability. (a) Top-30 rule retention between runs (dashed line: 90% threshold). (b) Rolling standard deviation of task accuracy (dotted lines: static baseline). High retention correlates with stable performance.

Figure 6:Hyperparameter selection for TACO. Left: effect of the top-kkrule retrieval size on accuracy and self-evolution token cost. Right: effect of batch sizeNNon accuracy and relative runtime. The self-evolution token cost and relative runtime shown in the figure are reported as relative values, normalized to the results withk=30k=30andN=4N=4.
